unit.name: Multimodal agents and current limitations
unit.description: Extends agents from DOM/text inputs to image inputs and ends with a candid assessment of current benchmark gaps and common failure modes.
unit.summary: The final segment motivates multimodal agents by arguing that feeding full HTML and JavaScript into context can be intractable, and that screenshots or pixels may sometimes be a better state representation for acting in interfaces [ts=3214s][ts=3249s]. It then sketches two vision-language models that have been adapted for agent use: LLaVA, which uses GPT-4-generated question-response supervision to jointly tune an image encoder and a text decoder, and Pix2Struct, which pretrains on website screenshots by masking regions and predicting the HTML of the masked elements [ts=3263s][ts=3441s]. The lecture emphasizes that current agent performance still depends heavily on bespoke prompting and few-shot examples, and that even on MiniWoB, long-horizon tasks degrade sharply as the number of required actions grows [ts=3461s][ts=3520s]. On harder settings such as WebArena and WebLINX, the gap to humans remains large, and models make trivial unrecoverable mistakes such as typing the email into the password field or repeating a search term multiple times; the recap then ties these limits back to the broader theme that both reasoning and agents remain open research problems [ts=3538s][ts=3812s].

key_points.text: For real interfaces, pixel or screenshot input can be preferable to feeding massive DOM and JavaScript contexts into the model.
LLaVA builds a vision-language model by using GPT-4-generated instruction-response pairs to jointly fine-tune an image encoder and a text decoder.
Pix2Struct pretrains on website screenshots by masking visual regions and training the decoder to output the HTML corresponding to the masked content.
Even with extensive prompting and few-shot examples, performance on MiniWoB drops as tasks require longer action sequences.
A representative agent failure is typing the email address into the password field and failing to recover.

It then sketches two vision-language models that have been adapted for agent use: LLaVA, which uses GPT-4-generated question-response supervision to jointly tune an image encoder and a text decoder, and Pix2Struct, which pretrains on website screenshots by masking regions and predicting the HTML of the masked elements [ts=3263s][ts=3441s]. The lecture emphasizes that current agent performance still depends heavily on bespoke prompting and few-shot examples, and that even on MiniWoB, long-horizon tasks degrade sharply as the number of required actions grows [ts=3461s][ts=3520s]. On harder settings such as WebArena and WebLINX, the gap to humans remains large, and models make trivial unrecoverable mistakes such as typing the email into the password field or repeating a search term multiple times; the recap then ties these limits back to the broader theme that both reasoning and agents remain open research problems [ts=3538s][ts=3812s].

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal UI agents
kp_catalog.description: Use vision-language models such as LLaVA and Pix2Struct so agents can act from screenshots or pixels rather than only text or DOM.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal UI agents clarifies use vision-language models such as LLaVA and Pix2Struct so agents can act from screenshots or pixels rather than only text or DOM, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Current limitations and human-model gap
kp_catalog.description: Despite progress, current reasoning and agent systems remain brittle, prompt-sensitive, and far below human reliability on longer tasks.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because current limitations and human-model gap synthesizes despite progress, current reasoning and agent systems remain brittle, prompt-sensitive, and far below human reliability on longer tasks into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Agent benchmarks and evaluation environments
kp_catalog.description: MiniWoB, WebArena, and WebLINX provide progressively more realistic settings for measuring task success in browser-like environments.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because agent benchmarks and evaluation environments clarifies miniWoB, WebArena, and WebLINX provide progressively more realistic settings for measuring task success in browser-like environments, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
